cards.front: How does negative feedback reduce gain?
cards.back: A_f = A/D where D = 1+Aβ. Gain is divided by the desensitivity factor D.
Closed-loop distortion = open-loop distortion / D. Feedback treats nonlinear distortion as just another error and corrects it.
BW_f = BW × D. Bandwidth is multiplied by D. Gain-bandwidth product is conserved.

cards.front: What happens to input impedance for series mixing?
cards.back: R_if = R_i × D. Series mixing (voltage) at input → increased input impedance. Moves toward ideal for voltage-input amplifiers.

cards.front: What happens to input impedance for shunt mixing?
cards.back: R_if = R_i / D. Shunt mixing (current) at input → decreased input impedance. Moves toward ideal for current-input amplifiers.

cards.front: What happens to output impedance for shunt sampling?
cards.back: R_of = R_o / D. Shunt sampling (voltage taking) at output → decreased output impedance. Ideal for voltage amplifiers.

cards.front: What happens to output impedance for series sampling?
cards.back: R_of = R_o × D. Series sampling (current sensing) at output → increased output impedance. Ideal for current/transconductance amplifiers.

cards.front: What is the gain sensitivity formula?
cards.back: dA_f/A_f = (1/D) × dA/A. If A changes by x%, A_f changes by x%/D. Gain is D times less sensitive to amplifier variations.

cards.front: Does feedback reduce noise injected at the input?
cards.back: Yes — input-referred noise is reduced by D, because the noise is treated identically to a signal at the input, passing through A_f = A/D instead of A.

cards.front: Why is gain-bandwidth product conserved with feedback?
cards.back: A_f × BW_f = (A/D)(BW × D) = A × BW. The D factors cancel. Trading gain for bandwidth preserves their product.

cards.front: What is the single cost of negative feedback?
cards.back: Reduced gain: A_f = A/D. For large loop gain (Aβ >> 1), you give up most of A to gain all the other benefits.

cards.front: Why does feedback stabilize gain against transistor variations when Aβ >> 1?
cards.back: When Aβ >> 1, A_f ≈ 1/β. β is set by stable passive resistors, not temperature-sensitive transistors. So A_f is stable even if transistor h_FE varies greatly.
